  One year
- **Obfuscate** (0); VDA 224
  To many common men and women living in the 13th century, the Devil lurks in every shadow and hides behind every corner. Evil is ever-present and a threat that can rarely be seen with your eyes.
  In the case of those Cainites who have mastered Obfuscate, this paranoia is justified. While evil may be debatable, Obfuscate makes it so that a gifted vampire could be lurking in the darkness ready to strike or flee at any time. The monster must be close at hand to accomplish this, no more than five meters per dots of Wits + Stealth from their would-be victims.
  Most mortals, even those trained in Awareness, cannot pierce the monster's deception. Some unique individuals may be able to see straight through these lies; children, horses, dogs, some cats, and very few religious visionaries are unaffected by Obfuscate, but this is all at Storyteller discretion.
  Cainite with the Discipline of Auspex may be able to pierce a Cainite's Obfuscate. For more, see Auspex on p. 195.
  Most Obfuscate powers last a scene once activated,unless willed away by the Cainite. They take no particular concentration to uphold once raised.
- **● The Silence of Death** (1); VDA 225
  There is no roll. The power creates a zone of absolute silence around the character, with a maximum radius of 7 meters. No sound occurs inside this zone, though sounds originating outside the area of effect may be heard by anyone in it. Additionally, if the player spends two blood points and the character spends five turns in concentration in a room or enclosure, the silence remains in it after the character leaves the room and lasts until she wills it to end. A character can't create more than one zone of silence, so if she wishes to use Silence of Death on herself (or curse another room), the zone in the first room fades automatically.
- **●● Aura Perception** (2); V20 135
  After the character stares at the subject for at least a few seconds, the player rolls Perception + Empathy (difficulty 8); each success indicates how much of the subject's aura the character sees and understands (see the table below). A failure indicates that the play of colors and patterns yields no prevailing impression. A botch indicates a false or erroneous interpretation. The Storyteller may wish to make this roll, thus keeping the player in the dark as to the veracity of the character's interpretation.
  
  **Successes**
  **Result**
  
  1 Success
  Can distinguish only the shade (pale or bright).
  
  2 Successes
  Can distinguish the main color.
  
  3 Successes
  Can recognize the color patterns.
  
  4 Successes
  Can detect subtle shifts.
  
  5 Successes
  Can identify mixtures of color and pattern.
  
  The Aura Colors chart offers some example ideas of common colors and the emotions they reflect that Storytellers can use. Note that it is nearly impossible to determine with certainty if a particular character is lying or not with this power - vampires are inherently deceitful by nature, but even mortals might react with anxiety to questions while still being truthful. It is, however, helpful in determine the target's emotional state, which might lead the vampire to decide that a particular target is suspicious.
  
  A character may choose to perform a very cursory aura scan of a large area like a nightclub's dance floor or the audience in a gallery. In this case, the player decides which characteristic of auras she's looking for, and that's the only information she's able to glean if the roll is successful. (At the Storyteller's discretion, on this general scan roll, more successes on the roll may more quickly yield what the character seeks.) For example, the player may specify, "Who's the most nervous person in attendance?" or "Are there any vampirically pale auras among the CEO's entourage?" Thereafter, the player may narrow down her scrutiny of a single individual, with an additional roll as normal.
  
  The character may focus in on a particular subject's aura only once per scene with any degree of clarity. Any subsequent attempts that result in failure should be considered botches. It is very easy for the character to imagine seeing what she wants to see when judging someone's intentions. After 24 hours, the character may try again at no penalty.
  
  It is possible, though difficult, to sense the aura of a being who is otherwise invisible to normal sight. Refer to "Seeing the Unseen," p. 142, for more information.
- **●● Unseen Presence** (2); VDA 225
  There is no roll necessary to conjure an Unseen Presence. Should a Cainite draw attention to herself by sneak attack, blunder, or speech, accidental attention will call for a Wits + Stealth roll, difficulty from 5 to 9, based on the amount of noise or calamity the Cainite has caused. Willful attempts to violate the secrecy of Unseen Presence while still maintaining it may call for a high difficulty and multiple successes. For example, whispering to an enemy without giving away her location would require three or so successes at a difficulty of 7.
  Even when seen or unseen, a secondary use of this power allows a Cainite to conceal small items already on her person, or with a successful Manipulation + Subterfuge from 5 to 9, based on the amount of observation she can grasp and conceal an easily moved item.
- **● Malleable Visage** (1); V20 241
  The player must spend a blood point for each body part to be changed, then roll Intelligence + Medicine (difficulty 6). To duplicate another person or voice requires a Perception + Medicine roll (difficulty 8), and five successes are required for a flawless copy; fewer successes leave minute (or not-so-minute) flaws. Increasing one's Appearance Trait has a difficulty of 9, and the vampire must spend an additional blood point for each dot of Appearance increased beyond their natural total. A botch permanently reduces the Attribute by one.
- **Vicissitude** (0); V20 241
  **Note:** Nosferatu _always_ "heal" Vicissitude alterations, at least the ones that make them better-looking. The ancient curse of the Clan may not be circumvented through Vicissitude. The same applies to physical deformities from the Gangrel Clan weakness.
  
  Body Crafts
  Vampires who wish to use Vicissitude well often specialize their knowledge of Medicine in an art known as Body Crafts. This specialization enables its possessor to make all manner of alterations to living and dead flesh and bone. It also gives insight into more mundane techniques; many Tzimisce are skilled at flaying, bone-carving, embalming, taxidermy, tattooing, and piercing.
- **Eyes of Chaos** (3); V20 - Dark Ages
  Fleeting clarity hides within insanity; a shattered mirror reflects more than one unbroken one. This level of Dementation is responsible for the Malkavians' reputation of gleaning hidden truths, and their tendency to speak these truths to power. She may scrutinize the "patterns" of personal interaction or even random events in nature itself to reveal hidden truths about the situation she's in. The character need not as a question - the pattern is devious at even a moment's glance.
  
  System: This power may only be used when scrutnizing a complex pattern or event, such as the interactions of a vampiric court or the clash of an army. Analysing the blood spatter flicked from one's fingers or the spill of entrails works just as well, however. Roll Perception + Enigmas, the difficulty varied based on available information and the campire's familiarity on available information and the vampire's familiarity with the situation. A court in the vampire's home culture and domain might be difficutly 5, while a foreign court (or the clash of two freign armies), to difficulty 7. A completely alien encounter (encountering mysterious Cathayan vampires) or just meeting an Anda would be difficulty 9.
  
  The players ask the Storytellers direct questions about the situation at hand. This can virtually be anything related to the hidden truth. The answers are related to the player directly and to the character via and to the characters via metaphors and imagery, with more successes providing a clearer and more complete answer. Failure produces imagery unrelated to the matter at hand, while a botch on the roll relates a completely untrue but nevertheless believable, which may explain some of the more radical Malkavian behavior. Viable questions may include, but not limited to:
  
  - A persons Nature and Demeanor.
  
  - What is the worst choice I could make?
  
  - What is the safest choice I could make?
  
  - Is this person being controlled by another?
  
  - What the underlying or hidden message?
  
  - How relevant are our overarching goals to this scene?
- **Pick a new out of clan discipline.**
  P

## Traits

- **Legerdemain** (0)
- **Archery** (0)
- **Commerce** (0)
- **Ride** (0)
- **Hearth Wisdom** (0)
- **Seneschal** (0)
- **Theology** (0)
- **Path of Christ (System)** (0)
  THE PATH OF CHRIST (CHRISTIANITY)
  Score Minimum Wrongdoing Rationale
  10 Failing to denounce corruption and sin All that evil needs to triumph is the inaction of the righteous.
  9 Acting out of pride, avarice, lust, or other
  sinful impulse
  The road to the Beast is paved in sin.
  8 Bearing false witness A lying tongue is hateful in the eyes of the Lord.
  7 Causing harm to a pious person God protects the innocent.
  6 Feeding from an innocent without permission Corruption of the innocent is a grave sin.
  5 Blasphemy or heresy in word or act Blasphemy is a sin against God.
  4 Murder of innocents Thou shalt not kill, sayeth the Lord.
  3 Worshiping false idols Thou shalt not have any other god before me.
  2 Aiding demons or supernatural evil It is the duty of the righteous to smite the wicked.
  1 Failing to spread the Gospel of Christ It is the duty of all Christians to spread the good news of our
  Lord

## Source

- Foundry world archive: aoe.zip
- Actor ID: 5ABlMK9Y8igRfLz3
- Actor folder: Players

## Session notes

- [[Game 1]] - uses visions and supernatural perception during the haven investigation.
- [[Game 2]] - is overwhelmed by nature spirits at [[Sabina Premysl's Cottage]] where the Gauntlet is extremely thin.

## Game 3-4 notes

- In [[Game 3]], Theodosia uses Dementation or Passion to deaden the gargoyle's emotional state.
- At [[Public Elysium]], she speaks with [[Khalid Mansur]].
- In [[Game 4]], she uses Passion to calm the Cappadocian negotiator long enough for the coterie to exit safely.

## Game 5-8 notes

- In [[Game 6]], Theodosia suffers [[Theodosia's Tzimisce Infection]] after praying over Radomir's stored blood.
- [[Ecaterina the Wise]] calms her and shares visions of the [[Promethean Dream]].
- In [[Game 7]], Theodosia's voice has changed and she can use Vicissitude; [[Dragan Premysl]] resets her voice.
- In [[Game 8]], she helps investigate the [[Impossible River]] and the Tremere parley aftermath.

## Chronicle 1 finale notes

- Looks into the [[Labyrinth Chasm]] and suffers spiritual damage, leaving her eyes black-on-black.
- Her tainted blood recoils during the [[First Fireflower Valderie]].
- In [[Game 14]], she interprets the land-spirits through a Christian theological lens rather than simply calling them pagan.
- Discussed as a possible abbot or seer figure in [[Pack Roles After Prague]], though her Tzimisce taint complicates leadership.

title: Theodosia's Tzimisce Infection
summary: Theodosia is infected or altered by Tzimisce Methuselah blood and develops Vicissitude-like effects.
text:
# Theodosia's Tzimisce Infection

## Overview

After praying over stored blood from Radomir's haven, [[Theodosia Byzantios]] senses the Tzimisce Methuselah presence in Prague. She claws at herself trying to remove it, later speaks in an unexpected masculine voice, and manifests Vicissitude.

## Confirmed Effects

- Theodosia perceives Shagra's seed in people, plants, blood, skin, and the city.
- She believes the ancient one saw her and might consume her soul.
- Her voice changes until [[Dragan Premysl]] uses Vicissitude to reset it.
- She can now use Vicissitude, though she frames it as infection rather than mastery.

## Related

- [[Shagra's Bloodline]]

text:
# Three Keys Tavern

## Overview

The Three Keys Tavern is a lively, rowdy tavern with music, darts, drinking, and card games.

## Important Events

- [[Dalibor]] investigates [[Wilhelm of the Three Keys]].
- [[Gregory]] shares intelligence during a card game.
- The coterie discusses transferring the tavern's fate through rigged chance.

title: Timeline
summary: Chronological notes from the first two Ashes of Ages sessions.
text:
# Timeline

## 1433 - Prague in crisis

- Jan Hus has been executed, but Hussite reform has become law in Bohemia.
- The Hussite cause is splintering between moderates and radicals while crusader pressure continues.
- Prague's Kindred court is tense under [[Prince Josef Zvi]] and [[Alessandro di Ravello]]. Younger vampires whisper about revolt and end-times prophecy.

## Game 1

- The coterie is summoned by Presence to [[Millie's Tavern]].
- [[Alessandro di Ravello]] announces the destruction of [[Radomir Pavlovich]] and orders the coterie to investigate.
- The coterie searches [[Radomir Pavlovich's Haven]].
- Evidence points to revenants, missing ritual texts, anti-Tremere correspondence, spirit protections, and the [[Stone of Hearts Wandering]].
- Father Zemislav removes the [[Bronze Fleshcrafted Vampire Statue]] from the haven.
- Dalibor arranges the purchase of the haven; [[Istvan Vlaszy]] is later identified as the losing bidder.
- A separate arson attempt occurs after the coterie leaves, and a suspect is taken to [[Castle Jail]].

## Game 2

- The group regroups at the [[Late Market]] and follows the bidder/courier lead to [[Istvan Vlaszy's House]].
- Istvan reveals a package route to a [[Remote Tzimisce Castle]] four hours north.
- The coterie visits [[Dragan Premysl's House]] and negotiates with Dragan.
- A watcher from the [[Neonate Elysium]] area attacks or warns them with a crossbow bolt tied to German/crusader lands.
- The group reaches [[Sabina Premysl's Cottage]], where the spirit world is dangerously close.
- Sabina reveals that the mystery package contained rituals and runes used in Radomir's spirit research.
- A gargoyle breaks through the roof as Sabina begins recreating the ritual.

## Game 3

- The coterie fights the gargoyle at [[Sabina Premysl's Cottage]] and [[Istvan Vlaszy]] destroys it.
- [[Sabina Premysl]] is pulled through the roof and carried north by an unidentified floating Cainite.
- A [[Shadow Creature at Sabina's Cottage]] says [[Radomir Pavlovich]] was killed by darkness and by someone connected to [[The Ones Who Kill]].
- At [[Public Elysium]], [[Dragan Premysl]] reveals [[Radomir's Cainite Sales to the Tremere]].
- A Tremere request for retroactive Embrace permission increases suspicion that Sabina was taken by Tremere.
- The crossbow trail leads to the [[Red Calf]], where Jakub discusses [[Blood Bond and the Crown]] and [[Fireflower and Proto-Vauldere]].

## Game 4

- [[Istvan Vlaszy]] delivers Radomir's package to a [[Remote Tzimisce Castle]] and sees [[Tremere-Hussite War Wagons]] attack.
- [[Eliska von Vlasak]] obtains the [[Fae Dream Fabric]].
- [[Father Zemislav]] witnesses the [[Hussite Crypt Penance Rite]].
- A Cappadocian negotiator makes the [[Cappadocian Cover-Up Offer]] and threatens Istvan with [[Mira Vlaszy's Ear]].
- [[Mira Vlaszy]] confirms [[Radomir's Cainite Sales to the Tremere]] and names the [[Golden Lane Shop]].
- At [[The Librarian's Library]], the coterie learns more about [[Prince Joseph and Antediluvian Influence]], [[Tremere Red Caps]], and [[Necromancer Countermeasures]].
- [[The Librarian]] arranges the [[Castle Catacombs Audience]] with [[Prince Josef Zvi]].

## Game 5

- [[Ari]] escorts the coterie to the [[Jewish Quarter Hidden Yeshiva]] for a private audience with [[Prince Josef Zvi]].
- The coterie identifies [[Jakub of Thessaloniki (Zakariya ibn Masud al-Muntazir)]] as [[Radomir Pavlovich]]'s killer.
- The Prince gives the [[Prince Josef's Abomination Commission]] and performs [[Prince Josef's Arm Ritual]] on [[Istvan Vlaszy]].
- [[Adrastos von Orlok]] explains [[Tremere as Prague Power Brokers]].
- On the rooftops, Jakub introduces the coterie to [[Graziano de Veronese]].

## Game 6

- [[Graziano de Veronese]] explains [[Graziano's Revolution]] and the [[Fireflower and Proto-Vauldere]].
- [[Dalibor]] pledges support; the others remain more cautious.
- [[Theodosia Byzantios]] suffers [[Theodosia's Tzimisce Infection]] after praying over Radomir's stored blood.
- The coterie meets [[Ecaterina the Wise]] and [[Cosimus]] at the [[Church Near the Night Market]].

## Game 7

- [[Dalibor]] begins working with the [[Mortal Sheriff of Prague]] and is pointed toward [[Wilhelm of the Three Keys]].
- [[Malina]] tests [[Jardani Jovonovich]] near the [[Night Market]].
- The group finds the [[Burned Salubri with the Third Eye]].
- [[Dragan Premysl]] reveals the [[Salubri Chantry Raid]] and the [[Wooden Heart-Seeking Creature]].
- At the [[Three Keys Tavern]], [[Gregory]] gives the [[Gregory's Raid Warning]] and names [[Adonis]].

## Game 8

- [[Father Zemislav]] spreads the [[Alessandro Hussite Extremist Rumor]].
- [[Eliska von Vlasak]] follows the [[Impossible River]] to the [[Butcher Shop of the Impossible River]].
- The coterie reaches the [[Tremere Public Chantry]] and speaks with [[Pierre de la Salle]].
- Pierre confirms [[Tremere as Garbage Men]] and offers occult exchanges.
- The coterie discusses the [[Elder Targets Plot]] and taking the Fireflower.

## Games 9-14

## Game 9

- [[Istvan Vlaszy]] warns his caravan through the [[Gypsy Caravan Escape Warning]].
- Istvan finds [[Borislav Premysl]] and [[Dragan's Szlachta Work]] at [[Dragan Premysl's House]].
- [[Katherine Karálová]] gathers [[Garinol Intelligence]].
- The coterie follows directions to the [[Hidden Salubri Cave]], learns about [[Salubri War Names]], and discovers the [[Labyrinth Chasm]].
- [[Carrion Footprints at the River]] suggest a corpse-like watcher followed them.

## Game 10

- [[Malina's Rune Sword]] replaces Istvan's weapon.
- Katherine rescues [[Rudolph the Clockwork Alchemist]].
- The coterie carries out the [[Orlok Assassination]] at the [[Three Keys Tavern]].
- Katherine hides [[Katherine's Hidden Trunk]] and receives the [[Stake the Librarian Offer]].
- The group flees to the [[Fireflower Anarch Camp]].

## Game 11

- The camp debates Istvan's place, leading to [[Istvan's Banu Haqim Embrace]].
- [[Father Zemislav]] creates the [[Silver Fireflower Chalice]] and leads the [[First Fireflower Valderie]].
- The Anarch leaders discuss [[Micah Vykos]], the [[Tremere Weapon Against Shagra]], [[Sabina as Tremere Leverage]], and the [[Gunpowder Diversion at Elder Elysium]].
- [[Katherine Karálová]] sees the [[Blue Comet Omen]].

## Game 12

- No raw Game 12 transcript was provided. See [[Game 12 Missing Transcript Summary]] for recap-only continuity notes.

## Game 13

- The coterie meets the [[Norse Setite Anteaters]]: [[Svigar Derkinson]], [[Ursa]], [[Colleen Norgobane]], and [[Thor Skydre]].
- The northern Setites discuss [[Cain's Northern Tomb]] and offer passage on the [[Setite Ship North]].
- A distant explosion marks the [[Gunpowder Diversion at Elder Elysium]].
- On the road, the group survives the [[Redcap Ambush on the Road]] and meets [[Greg the Tremere]].
- Greg demonstrates the [[Boat That Sailed on Land]] and gives the [[Tremere Origin Confession]].

## Game 14

- The pack crosses into the [[Land-Spirit Crossing]] and meets [[Devara]] and the [[Lithuanian Hearth Pact]].
- Devara's coven provides [[Hanged Men Blood Hospitality]].
- In the epilogue, the pack survives the [[Niktuku Attack in Estonia]], spreads its rites through [[St. Petersburg Sabbat Work]], and receives [[Helsinki and the Camarilla News]].
- At sea, Istvan becomes one of the [[Unconquered Banu Haqim]].
- The pack lands in [[Stockholm]], closing Chronicle 1. See [[Chronicle 1 Epilogue]].

## Reader Guide

- Start with [[Chronicle 1]] for the reading order.
- Use [[Chronicle 1 Summary]] for prose context.
- Use [[Chronicle 1 End State]] and [[Open Threads for Chronicle 2]] after reading the session summaries.

- A distant explosion marks the [[Gunpowder Diversion at Elder Elysium]].
- On the road, the group survives the [[Redcap Ambush on the Road]] and meets [[Greg the Tremere]].
- Greg demonstrates the [[Boat That Sailed on Land]] and gives the [[Tremere Origin Confession]].

## Game 14

- The pack crosses into the [[Land-Spirit Crossing]] and meets [[Devara]] and the [[Lithuanian Hearth Pact]].
- Devara's coven provides [[Hanged Men Blood Hospitality]].
- In the epilogue, the pack survives the [[Niktuku Attack in Estonia]], spreads its rites through [[St. Petersburg Sabbat Work]], and receives [[Helsinki and the Camarilla News]].
- At sea, Istvan becomes one of the [[Unconquered Banu Haqim]].
- The pack lands in [[Stockholm]], closing Chronicle 1. See [[Chronicle 1 Epilogue]].

## Reader Guide

- Start with [[Chronicle 1]] for the reading order.
- Use [[Chronicle 1 Summary]] for prose context.
- Use [[Chronicle 1 End State]] and [[Open Threads for Chronicle 2]] after reading the session summaries.

title: Tobias Doubravský
summary: Tobias Doubravský imported from the Foundry Ashes of Ages world (Unfiled Actors, Vampire). Vampire.
text:
# Tobias Doubravský

![Tobias Doubravský](/wiki/media/Tobias-Doubravsky.jpg)

## Overview

Tobias Doubravský imported from the Foundry Ashes of Ages world (Unfiled Actors, Vampire). Vampire.

## Foundry details

- **Actor type:** Vampire
- **Foundry folder:** Unfiled
- **Advantages:** Blood Pool 0/15

## Background

The has the appearance of a man in his mid-30s, as is common for his kind. His curly, dark brown hair frames a pale, angular face that often carries an air of calm contemplation. His beard, neatly trimmed and carefully maintained, adds to the regal presence he exudes, hinting at both wisdom and a controlled sense of danger.

Tobias dresses in the rich garments of a nobleman, befitting his status in the shadowy underworld of the Cainites. His attire is adorned with intricate patterns and brocade detailing, reminiscent of both the luxurious tastes of his former mortal life and the Ravnos clan's penchant for illusions and subtle deceptions. The deep burgundy and gold of his clothes speak of wealth, while the somber tones reflect the clandestine nature of his existence.

## Attributes

| Trait | Rating |
|---|---:|
| Strength | 2 |
| Dexterity (Slight of Hand) | 4 |
| Stamina | 2 |
| Charisma | 3 |
| Manipulation | 5 |
| Appearance | 2 |
| Perception | 2 |
| Intelligence | 2 |
| Wits | 2 |

## Abilities

**Talents:** Alertness 2, Athletics 3, Brawl 4, Expression 1, Subterfuge 2

**Skills:** craft 1, Etiquette 2, Melee 2, Stealth 3

**Knowledges:** Enigmas 2, Investigation 1

## Features, backgrounds, merits, and flaws

- **New background** (1)

## Disciplines and powers

- **Chimerstry** (0); VDA 204
- **● Ignis Fatuus** (1); VDA 204
  The player spends one Willpower point, and creating the illusions takes the vampire's action. The vampire can create illusions in an area of roughly 7 cubic meters per dot the vampire has in Chimerstry, and the vampire must be able to sense the area in which he creates the illusion. For example, he cannot create the illusion of a coin inside a box unless he can see or feel the inside of the box, but he can create the sound of coins rattling around if the box is shaken. The illusion remains until the vampire chooses to end the illusion or leaves the area.

## Traits

- **Legerdemain** (0)
- **Archery** (0)
- **Commerce** (0)
- **Ride** (0)
- **Hearth Wisdom** (0)
- **Seneschal** (0)
- **Theology** (0)

## Source

- Foundry world archive: aoe.zip
- Actor ID: gObudAwSOdIgDdKq
- Actor folder: Unfiled

title: Tremere as Garbage Men
summary: Pierre says the Tremere accept the role of converting unwanted vampires into useful servants.
text:
# Tremere as Garbage Men

## Overview

During the parley in [[Game 8]], [[Pierre de la Salle]] openly frames the Tremere as accepting a role like garbage men: taking unwanted Cainites from elders and turning them into something useful rather than merely destroying them.

## Confirmed By Pierre

- Multiple elders and clans have brought unwanted vampires to the Tremere.
- Dalibor's sire has brought broodmates to them.
- The right hand of the Prince and Father Zemislav's sire are also implicated in asking the Tremere for harsh justice.

## Related

- [[Radomir's Cainite Sales to the Tremere]]
- [[Tremere Public Chantry Parley]]

title: Tremere as Prague Power Brokers
summary: Adrastos says the Tremere cannot rule Prague openly, but can empower whoever does.
text:
# Tremere as Prague Power Brokers

## Overview

After the Prince's audience in [[Game 5]], [[Adrastos von Orlok]] tells the coterie that the Tremere are the court's major power brokers. No one would follow a Tremere prince, but whoever the Tremere support can force others to bow.

## Implications

- Tremere support has benefited other factions in different eras.
- Their war chest and preparation against the Tzimisce makes them difficult to oppose directly.
- Their role helps explain why the court tolerates behavior that younger Cainites find monstrous.

text:
# Tremere Castle Near Prague

## Overview

A Tremere-influenced castle or holding near the road associated with Istvan's courier knowledge. It is described as roughly three miles to the left if one takes the wrong road.

## Importance

- The location reinforces Tremere suspicion in the [[Radomir Pavlovich Investigation]].
- The transcript does not prove that the Tremere used this site in the murder.

title: Tremere-Hussite War Wagons
summary: Red-capped Hussite war wagons attack a Tzimisce courier castle after Istvan delivers Radomir's package.
text:
# Tremere-Hussite War Wagons

## Overview

After [[Istvan Vlaszy]] delivers Radomir's package to a [[Remote Tzimisce Castle]], four Hussite-style war wagons with red-capped soldiers attack the site.

## Confirmed Details

- War wagons are associated with Hussite military tactics.
- The red caps suggest Tremere-linked servants or messengers.
- Tzimisce at the castle believe the Tremere have blood-bound or manipulated some roving Hussite bands into attacking Tzimisce castles.

## Appears In

- [[Game 4]]

title: Tremere Origin Confession
summary: Greg's account of Tremere origins and altered Tzimisce vitae.
text:
# Tremere Origin Confession

## Overview

[[Greg the Tremere]] gives [[Katherine Karálová]] and the group a frank explanation of Tremere origins and hubris.

## Claims

- The Tremere compelled Tzimisce vitae into their own Embrace.
- They altered Tzimisce vitae to be independent of the spiritual Vicissitude taint.
- They then took power connected to the Salubri and inherited or inflicted part of that curse.
- Greg frames Tremere arrogance as real and dangerous, not merely a political accusation.

## Caution

These are Greg's claims. The transcript treats him as knowledgeable, but this page should not assume he gives a complete or unbiased history.

## Related

- [[Tremere-Tzimisce War]]
- [[Tremere as Prague Power Brokers]]
- [[Game 13]]

title: Tremere Plot Against Shagra
summary: Hub for the Tremere weapon, Sabina leverage plan, and Shagra's flesh-room threat.
text:
# Tremere Plot Against Shagra

## Overview

Late in Chronicle 1, the Anarch leaders reveal that the Tremere have built a weapon to strike the ancient Tzimisce force associated with [[Shagra's Bloodline]]. The pack considers whether [[Sabina Premysl]] can be used as leverage to make the Tremere deploy it before they flee Prague.

## Key Pages

- [[Tremere Weapon Against Shagra]]
- [[Sabina as Tremere Leverage]]
- [[Shagra's Bloodline]]
- [[Tremere Public Chantry]]
- [[Gunpowder Diversion at Elder Elysium]]
- [[What Happened to Prague]]

## Outcome

The transcript does not show the plan being completed. The pack leaves Prague while the [[Night of Chaos]] unfolds.

title: Tremere Public Chantry
summary: The public Tremere Chantry front used for knowledge exchange and parley.
text:
# Tremere Public Chantry

## Overview

The public Tremere Chantry appears as an apothecary and curiosity shop during the day. It includes a parlor used for formal meetings.

## Important Events

- [[Pierre de la Salle]] meets the coterie there in [[Game 8]].
- The Tremere use it to exchange knowledge and information with other clans.

## Chronicle 1 finale notes

- The Anarch plan in [[Game 11]] centers on forcing or luring the Tremere to use the [[Tremere Weapon Against Shagra]].
- [[Sabina as Tremere Leverage]] is discussed because Sabina's off-screen Tremere Embrace may let her argue that Shagra can pursue the Tremere even if they flee Prague.

title: Tremere Public Chantry Parley

text:
# Tremere Public Chantry Parley

## Overview

In [[Game 8]], the coterie emerges near or within the [[Tremere Public Chantry]] and is brought into an apothecary/curiosity-shop front. [[Pierre de la Salle]] speaks for House and Clan Tremere.

## Topics

- The Tremere ask whether the coterie ordered or encouraged [[Dragan Premysl]]'s Chantry raid.
- Pierre offers exchanges of occult knowledge.
- He presents the Tremere as useful to many elders and clans.
- He confirms that the Tremere transform unwanted vampires into loyal or useful servants.
- He claims the Salubri are infernalists and says the Tremere seek their destruction.

## Appears In

- [[Game 8]]

title: Tremere Red Caps
summary: Red-capped messengers and servants are tied to Tremere mortal support networks rather than changeling redcaps.
text:
# Tremere Red Caps

## Overview

Research at [[The Librarian's Library]] identifies red caps as Tremere-associated servants or messengers. The name derives from old mage/Ars Magica traditions of messengers wearing red caps for luck, later caught in Clan Tremere's orbit.

## Confirmed Details

- The red caps seen with Hussite war wagons are not identified as fae Redcaps.
- Their association points toward Tremere influence among mortal troops and messengers.
- This helps explain red-capped Hussite forces attacking Tzimisce holdings.

## Appears In

- [[Game 4]]

## Game 5-8 notes

- In [[Game 5]], Istvan clarifies that the red caps he saw were a contingent of Tremere mortal resources, with active mages among some units.

title: Tremere-Tzimisce War
summary: The war and suspicion surrounding Radomir's correspondence.
text:
# Tremere-Tzimisce War

## Confirmed facts

- Radomir's correspondence contains repeated aggression toward Clan Tremere and arguments that they should be purged.
- Radomir pressed [[Prince Josef Zvi]] to expel the Tremere from Prague.
- The prince refused to facilitate such violence and stated that he had given the Tremere domain.
- Radomir was searching for the Tremere's primary chantry.
- In Game 2, Istvan recognizes that a route connected to Radomir's packages passes near a Tremere-influenced castle area.

## Unconfirmed

- The transcripts make Tremere involvement a strong suspicion, but do not prove that Tremere killed Radomir.

## Game 3-4 notes

- Dragan and Mira reveal that Radomir secretly traded Cainites or bodies to the Tremere for war magic and esoteric knowledge.
- Sabina's suspected Tremere abduction and the red-capped war wagons attacking a Tzimisce castle both escalate the conflict.
- Game 4 research identifies the red caps as Tremere-linked servants or messengers.

title: Tremere Weapon Against Shagra
summary: The weapon the Tremere built to strike Shagra's ancient psyche.
text:
# Tremere Weapon Against Shagra

## Overview

At the [[Fireflower Anarch Camp]], the Anarch leaders explain that the Tremere have built a weapon intended to strike the psyche of the ancient Tzimisce force associated with [[Shagra's Bloodline]].

## Details

- The weapon is known by three Tremere.
- It can be empowered by Tremere and carried to the target location.
- The likely target location is the flesh room or corpse-work site associated with Shagra's presence in Prague.
- The coterie considers using [[Sabina as Tremere Leverage]] to convince the Tremere to detonate or deploy the weapon before fleeing.

## Related

- [[Tremere Public Chantry]]
- [[Shagra's Bloodline]]
- [[Sabina Premysl]]
- [[Game 11]]

title: Tzimisce Revenant Network
summary: How Radomir and the Tzimisce use revenant families in Prague.
text:
# Tzimisce Revenant Network

## Confirmed facts

- Radomir used revenants as both protectors and messengers.
- Revenant blood is supernatural and does not decay like normal mortal blood in the same way.
- The coterie learns the Premysl family served Radomir closely.
- [[Dragan Premysl]] acted as an enforcer/protector.
- [[Sabina Premysl]] served as a spirit-vessel in Radomir's research.
- [[Istvan Vlaszy]] served as a courier for Radomir's packages.

## Notes

- The transcripts state that revenants primarily serve the Tzimisce, though some may be traders or otherwise outside direct household service.
- If no Tzimisce claims Radomir's revenants, they may become available to those with the means to employ or protect them.

title: Unconquered Banu Haqim
summary: Istvan survives the Assamite blood curse through the pack's Viniculum.
text:
# Unconquered Banu Haqim

## Overview

During the voyage to [[Stockholm]], [[Istvan Vlaszy]] feels the pressure of the Tremere curse placed on the Assamites/Banu Haqim.

## Event

- Istvan feels compelled to submit.
- He fights it.
- He coughs up all his blood as a viscous, spiky creature.
- The pack restrains and feeds him while others fight the manifested curse off the boat.
- The Viniculum protects him where many Banu Haqim without such bonds find vitae poisonous.

## Meaning

Istvan is described as one of the Unconquered: broken from old bindings, but unbound and unbroken by the Tremere curse.

## Related

- [[Istvan's Banu Haqim Embrace]]

playerText:
# Unconquered Banu Haqim

## Overview

During the voyage to [[Stockholm]], [[Istvan Vlaszy]] feels the pressure of the Tremere curse placed on the Assamites/Banu Haqim.

## Event

- Istvan feels compelled to submit.
- He fights it.
- He coughs up all his blood as a viscous, spiky creature.
- The pack restrains and feeds him while others fight the manifested curse off the boat.
- The Viniculum protects him where many Banu Haqim without such bonds find vitae poisonous.

## Meaning

Istvan is described as one of the Unconquered: broken from old bindings, but unbound and unbroken by the Tremere curse.

## Related

- [[Istvan's Banu Haqim Embrace]]
- [[First Fireflower Valderie]]
- [[Game 14]]

title: What Happened to Prague
summary: Prague's political and supernatural aftermath after the coterie's revolt.
text:
# What Happened to Prague

## Before The Collapse

Prague begins as a tense city under [[Prince Josef Zvi]], [[Alessandro di Ravello]], and elder supervision. The Hussite crisis gives mortal cover to supernatural panic, and [[Radomir Pavlovich]]'s destruction exposes Tremere, Tzimisce, revenant, and spirit conflicts beneath the city.

## Breaking Points

- [[Sabina Premysl]] is taken and later Embraced by the Tremere off screen.
- The coterie exposes Cainite sales to the Tremere.
- The Fireflower gives younger vampires a way to escape elder blood bonds.
- Hidden Salubri aid makes the [[Orlok Assassination]] possible.
- The [[Gunpowder Diversion at Elder Elysium]] escalates the Anarch assault.

## Aftermath

- [[Adrastos von Orlok]] is dead.
- Toreador, Lasombra, Cappadocian, and other elder networks are destabilized.
- Shagra's influence remains a threat rather than a solved problem.
- The coterie leaves before seeing the full city outcome.
- Later news in [[Game 14]] says the Anarchs lose the wider war and the Camarilla forms.

title: What the Pack Knows
summary: A reader-facing inventory of major truths the pack carries out of Prague.
text:
# What The Pack Knows

## About Prague

- The city's elder order was fragile before the coterie struck.
- [[Radomir Pavlovich]]'s death was not an isolated murder.
- The Tremere were buying, transforming, and weaponizing Cainites.
- The Tzimisce revenant network is tied to older land and blood influences.

## About The Fireflower

- The Fireflower can break elder blood bonds.
- It can create a new bond between participants.
- The rite is not merely political; it produces visions and spiritual consequences.
- [[First Fireflower Valderie]] is the pack's founding rite.

## About Shagra And Land

- Shagra's influence works through flesh, blood, revenants, and possibly the land itself.
- The Tremere have a weapon designed to strike Shagra's psyche.
- [[Devara]]'s coven shows that vampires can relate to land-spirits through mutual pacts instead of domination.

## About The North

- The [[Norse Setite Anteaters]] carry Fireflower north.
- [[Cain's Northern Tomb]] may exist, but its truth is unconfirmed.
- [[Stockholm]] is the next meaningful stage of the pack's story.

title: Wilhem Streicher
summary: Wilhem Streicher imported from the Foundry Ashes of Ages world (NPC Roster, Vampire). Vampire.
text:
# Wilhem Streicher

![Wilhem Streicher](/wiki/media/Wilhem-Streicher.jpg)

## Overview

Wilhem Streicher imported from the Foundry Ashes of Ages world (NPC Roster, Vampire). Vampire.

## Foundry details

- **Actor type:** Vampire
- **Foundry folder:** NPC's
- **Advantages:** Blood Pool 0/10

## Background

**Wilhem Streicher, the Stoic Flame of the Brujah**

Wilhem Streicher was born in the late 13th century in the small Bavarian town of Regensburg, a place often caught in the crossfire of noble disputes and plagued by raids from brigands. The son of a blacksmith, he learned early the power of patience and precision--a necessity for someone who handled burning metal daily. Despite his harsh surroundings, Wilhem had a calmness that set him apart from other young men. He possessed an unusual capacity to bide his time and listen, a trait rare among the impetuous and quick-tempered Brujah, and this earned him the quiet respect of his community. It was only during true moments of injustice that Wilhem would unleash his fury, often with devastating efficiency.

In his mortal life, Wilhem became known as a defender of the downtrodden, assisting townsfolk oppressed by local nobility or marauding thieves. His reputation eventually attracted the attention of a local Ventrue lord, who saw his skill and influence as a threat to his authority. But, while the Ventrue aimed to snuff out this troublesome mortal, a traveling Brujah named Ecaterina sensed in Wilhem a kindred soul--a steadfastness that could serve the Brujah's ideals. She was impressed by his self-control and sense of duty, qualities she saw as a rare foundation for Brujah rebellion.

**The Embrace and Service to Ecaterina**

Wilhem was Embraced in 1302 by Ecaterina, who believed that under her guidance, he would become a valuable asset to her ongoing struggles. She sought to temper his unusual serenity with the Brujah's innate passion, creating a vampire whose steady hand could support her often chaotic cause. In those early years, he acted as her servant, a role he accepted willingly as he learned to navigate his new existence as a Cainite. While many Brujah chafed under the constraints of servitude, Wilhem's patience and respect for Ecaterina kept him grounded.

During his years with her, Wilhem took on a variety of roles: guard, advisor, and occasionally an assassin. His demeanor was practical and restrained, yet his strength and loyalty proved invaluable, especially during battles against Ventrue and Tzimisce. Known for his ability to strike with a cool head even in the heat of battle, Wilhem was often entrusted with missions that required a clear mind and steady hand. His calmness became a hallmark among his peers, a source of curiosity for other Brujah who struggled to harness their rage.

**Philosophical Divergence and Independent Ideals**

Wilhem, while committed to the Brujah cause of justice, began to grow uncomfortable with the more chaotic and indiscriminate violence that he often witnessed among his clan. Though he respected Ecaterina and understood her fury, he sought to channel his own sense of justice in a different way. Over the years, he developed a personal philosophy: strength should only be wielded when necessary, and violence should serve a purpose beyond mere vengeance.

This calm and thoughtful approach set Wilhem apart as something of a "Brujah outsider" within his own clan, who struggled with their hot-blooded reputation. Yet he remained fiercely loyal to Ecaterina, viewing her as the only Brujah elder who truly understood his convictions. This loyalty came from more than blood ties; he admired her resilience, finding in her a model for his own ideals of controlled power.

**1433 Prague and Wilhem's Continued Role**

Now, in 1433, as Prague becomes a nexus of power struggles and revolts, Wilhem walks a careful path. The city's conflicts, both mortal and Cainite, serve as a reminder of his past struggles and fuel his commitment to Ecaterina's cause. He has established a reputation as a mediator among Kindred, though his Brujah lineage ensures he is never underestimated. In a court rife with political intrigue, he maintains a distance, preferring to act as an observer and tactician, offering his counsel to those he deems worthy of trust.

However, despite his composed exterior, Wilhem carries a burning resolve: to find a way to guide his clan beyond the cycles of rebellion that often lead to ruin. He works quietly within Prague, seeking alliances and building a network of like-minded Brujah, Gangrel, and even Toreador who share his vision of change through deliberate action rather than reckless fury.

**Legacy and Future**

Wilhem's approach to Brujah ideals--justice, freedom, and reason--has turned him into an unlikely icon among Prague's younger Kindred, many of whom seek him out for guidance. Some consider him a Brujah who has lost his way, while others admire his unshakable calm as an evolution of Brujah philosophy. Whatever the view, Wilhem is known as a Cainite who sees himself as much a protector as he is a warrior, a vampire willing to shoulder the burdens of mentorship and leadership without seeking personal power.

As Prague simmers on the brink of change, Wilhem's role as an ally, mentor, and quiet tactician becomes crucial. While he may not seek to shape the world through force, he knows that with the right guidance and alliances, he could help mold Prague into a new symbol of Kindred unity--a haven for those willing to embrace the Brujah ideals without succumbing to chaos.

Though he may forever remain an unusual Brujah, Wilhem Streicher is certain of one thing: his place is at Ecaterina's side and on the path to justice, wherever that path may lead.

## Attributes

| Trait | Rating |
|---|---:|
| Strength | 1 |
| Dexterity | 1 |
| Stamina | 1 |
| Charisma | 1 |
| Manipulation | 1 |
| Appearance | 1 |
| Perception | 1 |
| Intelligence | 1 |
| Wits | 1 |

## Traits

- **Legerdemain** (0)
- **Archery** (0)
- **Commerce** (0)
- **Ride** (0)
- **Hearth Wisdom** (0)
- **Seneschal** (0)
- **Theology** (0)

## Source

- Foundry world archive: aoe.zip
- Actor ID: QEhzLoHZrowec3zW
- Actor folder: NPC's

## Chronicle 1 finale notes

- Speaks with [[Jardani Jovonovich]] at the [[Night Market]] in [[Game 10]] about the Nosferatu curse, Cain, and brotherhood.

title: Yahya al-Qamar
summary: Yahya al-Qamar imported from the Foundry Ashes of Ages world (Elders, Vampire). Vampire.
text:
# Yahya al-Qamar

![Yahya al-Qamar](/wiki/media/Yahya-al-Qamar.png)

## Overview

Yahya al-Qamar imported from the Foundry Ashes of Ages world (Elders, Vampire). Vampire.

## Foundry details

- **Actor type:** Vampire
- **Foundry folder:** NPC's / Elders
- **Foundry name note:** Malk
- **Original Foundry name:** Yahya al-Qamar (Malk)
- **Advantages:** Willpower 3; Blood Pool 0/14

## Background

Born in the Arabian Peninsula in 985 AD, Yahya al-Qamar was a Bedouin, traveling across the deserts of Arabia as a devout Muslim. He followed the Prophet's teachings closely and became known for his wisdom and knowledge of the faith. As a wanderer, he found himself welcomed in the courts of the Caliphate and noble households of Al-Andalus and the Middle East, where he shared his insights as both a spiritual advisor and a scholar. His knowledge of theology and philosophy made him respected, but it also stirred in him a deeper hunger for understanding the divine, one that led him on a fateful journey to Constantinople in the early 11th century.

In 1008, while in Constantinople, Yahya encountered a Malkavian elder who was captivated by his fervent search for truth and the divine. This elder, seeing a kindred spirit in Yahya's endless quest to understand God, decided to Embrace him, believing that immortality would allow Yahya to delve deeper into his search for divine knowledge. The Embrace, however, shattered Yahya's world. While it granted him the immortality to pursue wisdom, it also fractured his mind. His insight deepened, but so did his paranoia and delusions. Yahya soon had a religious revelation that caused him to convert to Christianity, believing that Christ, rather than the Prophet, held the ultimate key to understanding God's plan.

After his conversion, Yahya joined a Christian monastery, thinking that the monastic life would bring him closer to divine revelation. He spent years, decades even, in quiet contemplation, studying scripture and trying to understand the mysteries of God. However, instead of bringing him peace, this life only deepened his paranoia. He began to believe that God was constantly sending him messages--signs and visions--but that he was failing to interpret them correctly. This fear gnawed at him, driving him to the edge of madness. Yahya became obsessed with deciphering these divine messages, convinced that he was part of some grand, cosmic plan but eternally unsure of his role within it.

To quell his paranoia, Yahya began embracing individuals who claimed to have prophetic abilities--seers, mystics, and visionaries--hoping that through them, he could find the clarity he so desperately sought. These childer were meant to act as conduits between him and God, interpreting the messages that Yahya believed were being sent his way. Yet, every decade or so, the revelations they brought him would fail to satisfy his ever-growing need for understanding. Each failure drove him deeper into madness, as his belief that he was missing some crucial aspect of the divine plan consumed him. He would abandon his childer, moving on to find new prophets to embrace, but none could grant him the answers he sought.

In 1430, feeling once again restless and unfulfilled, Yahya traveled north, leaving behind the monasteries of Byzantium to seek out new forms of wisdom among the witches and seers of the Slavic lands. He believed that the magic and ancient traditions of these lands might offer him a new perspective on God's messages. However, as he wandered through Bohemia, Yahya stumbled upon a **Hussite communion**, a gathering that would change his path once again. In their radical reinterpretation of Christian doctrine and their rebellion against the Catholic Church, Yahya saw echoes of the divine confusion he felt. The Hussites, with their rejection of established authority, resonated with Yahya's belief that God's truth was being misinterpreted and that the established order was blind to the true messages from above.

Convinced that Prague, the heart of the Hussite movement, was where God intended him to be, Yahya settled in the city in 1433. He became fascinated with the Hussites' interpretation of Christian scripture, feeling that perhaps their struggle was the key to unlocking the mystery of God's messages. He interpreted the Hussite rebellion as a sign that Prague was the epicenter of a divine plan he had to uncover. His paranoia deepened once more, as he believed the city itself held the answers he had been searching for across centuries. The religious upheaval and the tensions between Catholicism and the Hussites fed into his belief that this was the battleground where God's true intentions would finally be revealed to him.

To aid in his search, Yahya has begun calling his scattered childer back to Prague. He believes that together, they can piece together the signs and messages that have plagued him for so long. However, many of his childer, having endured decades or even centuries of his madness, are wary of returning. Those who do answer his summons find a Yahya more paranoid and obsessed than ever, convinced that his role in God's plan is on the verge of being revealed. They are drawn into his world of prophetic visions, religious zeal, and divine confusion, as Yahya continues his relentless quest to uncover the truth hidden behind God's cryptic messages.

## Attributes

| Trait | Rating |
|---|---:|
| Strength | 1 |
| Dexterity | 1 |
| Stamina | 3 |
| Charisma | 1 |
| Manipulation | 5 |
| Appearance | 2 |
| Perception | 5 |
| Intelligence | 2 |
| Wits | 3 |

## Abilities

**Talents:** Alertness 3, Athletics 1, Awareness 3, Empathy 4, Expression 1, Intimidation 1, Leadership 2, Subterfuge 2

**Skills:** Etiquette 2, Stealth 2, Survival 2

**Knowledges:** Academics 2, Enigmas 4, Investigation 2, Occult 2

## Disciplines and powers

- **Auspex** (0)
  Sudden Revelations
  
  Characters with even one level of Auspex may occasionally get a sense of an impending threat against them. While they are otherwise using any level of Auspex, the Storyteller may choose to roll for a sudden revelation. In secret, she rolls the character's Auspex score. The difficulty varies by Storyteller discretion, as a robber behind an inn door would probably be difficulty 4, while a plot by the Prince's childe to destroythe character that would take decades to unravel would be difficulty 9. Difficulty can also reflect how important it is to the story that the character has a glimpse of what's coming. Keep in mind though, it is only a brief precognitive glimpse that may or may not make sense before the event unfurls. Divination is not a science; express the revelation in small sensory details or simple gut feelings. "You smell fire with no source coming from the farm house" or "he can't be trusted alone" are excellent examples
- **Obfuscate** (0)
- **Dementation** (0)
  If madness shatters the soul, Dementation is the hammer to the fracture point. If madness frees the mind, Dementation is the key to the cage. The Discipline allows a vampire to focus insanity and channel it to those around them. Malkavian clan elders claim the conscious mind is a pleasing and soothing mask, and that the Discipline never creates a madness that wasn't already there. Instead, it crumbles the barriers of self-control and consciousness to unleash the deep, dark insanity that dwells beneath. Viewed in this light, Dementation does not corrupt the mind ofthe victim--it unmasks their mind's truth for all to see.
  
  Each level of Dementation may only be used once per night on a victim. While Malkavians are the natural masters of this Discipline, it's usable by any vampire who possesses a Derangement. Vampires learning Dementation will gradually develop a Derangement; if this Derangementis cured by means magical or mundane, their knowledge of Dementation becomes unusable academia. Dwelling on this knowledge presages a relapse into madness.
- **Heightened Senses** (1)
  This power costs nothing, is reflexive, and requires no rolls to use. When activated, any roll related to the sense's use is decreased in difficulty by the character's Auspex dots. A character may utilize one sense at a time, or all of them in any combination she likes.This power does not allow the character to see in perfect darkness as with Eyes of the Beast (see p. 240).
- **Read the Soul** (2)
  The soul-gazer must actively and attentively observe her subject, allowing the sense of the soul to wash over her. At the moment she chooses to interpret the state of the soul, the character's player rolls Perception + Empathy (difficulty 8), with each success granting the character more pieces of information about their subject. Consider the chart below for ideas as to what information the Storyteller might communicate.
  
  A failure indicates that the character was unable to interpret the current state of the soul. A botch indicates misinformation or a wrong interpretation. Soul-gazing is not a science.The chart on the right consists of colors and their suggested interpretations. Interpretations through other senses are also possible. Players and Storytellers should be encouraged to create charts for their characters, unique in its sensory details.
  
  1 success Can distinguish only the shade (pale orbright).
  
  2 successes  Can distinguish the main color.
  
  3 successes  Can recognize the color patterns.
  
  4 successes  Can detect subtle shifts.
  
  5 successes  Can identify mixtures of color and pattern.
  
  The ability to Read the Soul does not act as a lie-detector, and absolute truths cannot be determined. It just reveals moments of dishonesty, or flooded feelings of guilt. An exceptional liar may be free of guilt, and a penitent but innocent person may always appear on the edge ofsuspicion. A talented Auspex user knows this, and will keep it in mind when interpreting what she perceives.
  
  An Auspex user may use Read the Soul to search a crowd or scan an area. She starts out by declaring what she is looking for, such as "a soul stained with Amaranth" or "the most nervous person in the room." Her player then rolls as normal. The information is general, and anymore intimate study of a target's soul requires a new roll
- **Spirit's Touch** (3)
  A player rolls Perception + Empathy. Difficulty varies by age and strength of impression. A stake found in a pile of ash just an hour old may be difficulty 4, a stake that had been in a vampire killed five days ago might be a difficulty 6, and a stake that only carried intent but was never used may yield useful impressions only at a difficulty 9.
  
  Difficulty should only be considered when a challenge and possible failure would add to the story. Sometimes,a Storyteller might choose to forgo a roll entirely when acharacter attempts to touch an object that is vital to the continuing story or a piece of strong setting importance. If the Storyteller needs characters to know they have a fang pulled from a Methuselah's mouth, or the sword that decapitated the Sheriff, she should forgo the roll and bring on the terrifying visions.

  2 successes  A second piece of basic information.
  
  3 successes  More useful information about the last owner, such as age and state of mind the last time he used the item.
  
  4 successes  The person's name.
  
  5+ successes  A wealth of information is available; nearly anything you want to know about theperson's relationship with that object.
- **Incubus Humor** (1)
  Roll Manipulation + Empathy against adifficulty of the target's Road score (for vampires) orWillpower (for mortals). Each success extends the duration of the inflamed humor. Effects might include one- or two-point changes to difficulties of frenzy or Virtue rolls, rolls to resist Presence powers, etc. Respite comes only if the humor imbalance is properly diagnosed and treated (Intelligence + Medicine, difficulty 8) -- every successon the treatment roll cancels one of the Malkavian's duration successes

- **Soul Haunting** (2)
  The vampire spends three turns speaking tothe victim, after which the player spends a blood pointand rolls Manipulation + Empathy (difficulty of his victim's Perception + Self-Control/Instinct). The number of successes determines the length of the visions.

  2 successes  Two nights
  
  3 successes  One week
  
  4 successes  One month
  
  5 successe  Three months
  
  6+ successes  One year
  
  Precisely when they manifest or what form the vision stake is up to the Storyteller, but they inflict a -2 penalty to all dice pools for two turns after manifestation.
- **Eyes of Chaos** (3)
  This power may only be used when scrutinizing a complex pattern or event, such as the interactions of a vampiric court or the clash of an army. Analyzing the blood spatter flicked from one's fingers or the spill of entrails works just as well, however. Roll Perception + Enigmas; the difficulty varies based on available information and the vampire's familiarity with the situation. A court in the vampire's home cultureand domain might be difficulty 5, while a foreign court (or the clash of two foreign armies) is difficulty 7. A completely alien encounter (encountering one of the mysterious Cathayan vampires, or even just meeting an Anda) would be difficulty 9. The player asks the Storyteller a direct question about the situation at hand. This can be virtually anything related to hidden truths. The answers are related to the player directly and to the character via metaphors and imagery, with more successes providing a clearer and more complete answer. Failure produces imagery unrelated tothe matter at hand, while a botch on the roll relates a completely untrue but nevertheless believable answer, which may explain some of the more radical Malkavian behavior. Viable questions include, but are not limited to:
  
  •What's this person's Nature and Derangements?
  
  •What's the worst choice I could make?
  
  •What's the safest choice?
  
  •Is this person being controlled by another?
  
  •What's the underlying or hidden message here?
  
  •How relevant to our overarching goals is this scene?
- **Fire Voice** (4)
  This power is a focused, more potent SoulHaunting. The vampire still rolls Manipulation + Empathy (difficulty 7); success on the roll now allows the vampire to affect one target per success, although all potential victims must still be listening to the vampire's voice for three turns. Affected victims immediately fly into a Rötschreck-like blind fear. Vampires (or other creatures capable of frenzy, such as lupines) may make a Rötschreck test (Storyteller's choice as to how others are affected) at +2 difficulty to resist the power. Mortals are automatically affected and don't remember their actions while frightened. The frenzy or fear lasts for a scene, with the Soul Haunting effect lasting for an additional night thereafter (and with especially fiery imagery). Vampires and others may test as usual to break their frenzy.
  
  The vampire using Fire Voice must also test for Rötschreck upon invoking this power, though his difficulty to resist is one lower than normal. If the initial roll to invoke this power is a failure, however, the difficulty of the roll to resist the frenzy increases by one instead. If the roll to invoke this power is a botch, the Rötschreck response is automatic.
- **Shedding the Mask** (5)
  The vampire must gain her target's undivided attention for at least one full turn to enact this power. The player spends a blood point and rolls Manipulation + Intimidation (difficulty of her victim's current Willpower points). If the roll is successful, the victim is afflicted with five Derangements -- a Lunacy Derangement and the Four Humor imbalances. The number of successes determines the duration.

  3 successes  One week
  
  4 successes  One month
  
  5+ successesOne year
  
  On a botch, the vampire suffers all of the Derangements intended for the victim. A minimum of six Derangements may well reduce the vampire to catatonic torpor.The victim (or the target of a botch) can spend a number of Willpower points equal to the successes rolled to end the duration prematurely.
- **The Silence of Death** (1)
  There is no roll. The power creates a zone of absolute silence around the character, with a maximum radius of 7 meters. No sound occurs inside this zone, though sounds originating outside the area of effect maybe heard by anyone in it. Additionally, if the player spends two blood points and the character spends five turns inconcentration in a room or enclosure, the silence remainsin it after the character leaves the room and lasts until she wills it to end. A character can't create more than one zone of silence, so if she wishes to use Silence of Death on herself (or curse another room), the zone in the first room fades automatically.

  (Perhaps a famous theologian he met while studying.)

  This is not your native land; this is not your mother tongue. You are not from around here. Everything is strange and different, from the customs to the food. You struggle with the area's native language (though you can get by with a purchase of the Lingua Franca Merit) and the difficulty to applicable Social rolls increases by one.
- **Flaw: Initiate of the Road 1** (0)
  (My thought is his quest to learn divine truths might have him flucuating between different Roads at times.)
- **Merit: Unbondable 3** (0)
  The stilled blood within you refuses to be compelled. You cannot be bound via the Blood Oath. This does not render you immune to any other source of mental influence, however. If your character is of Clan Tremere, you may not take this Merit

playerText:
# Yahya al-Qamar

![Yahya al-Qamar](/wiki/media/Yahya-al-Qamar.png)

## Overview

Yahya al-Qamar imported from the Foundry Ashes of Ages world (Elders, Vampire). Vampire.

## Foundry details

- **Actor type:** Vampire
- **Foundry folder:** NPC's / Elders
- **Foundry name note:** Malk
- **Original Foundry name:** Yahya al-Qamar (Malk)
- **Advantages:** Willpower 3; Blood Pool 0/14

## Background

Born in the Arabian Peninsula in 985 AD, Yahya al-Qamar was a Bedouin, traveling across the deserts of Arabia as a devout Muslim. He followed the Prophet's teachings closely and became known for his wisdom and knowledge of the faith. As a wanderer, he found himself welcomed in the courts of the Caliphate and noble households of Al-Andalus and the Middle East, where he shared his insights as both a spiritual advisor and a scholar. His knowledge of theology and philosophy made him respected, but it also stirred in him a deeper hunger for understanding the divine, one that led him on a fateful journey to Constantinople in the early 11th century.

In 1008, while in Constantinople, Yahya encountered a Malkavian elder who was captivated by his fervent search for truth and the divine. This elder, seeing a kindred spirit in Yahya's endless quest to understand God, decided to Embrace him, believing that immortality would allow Yahya to delve deeper into his search for divine knowledge. The Embrace, however, shattered Yahya's world. While it granted him the immortality to pursue wisdom, it also fractured his mind. His insight deepened, but so did his paranoia and delusions. Yahya soon had a religious revelation that caused him to convert to Christianity, believing that Christ, rather than the Prophet, held the ultimate key to understanding God's plan.

After his conversion, Yahya joined a Christian monastery, thinking that the monastic life would bring him closer to divine revelation. He spent years, decades even, in quiet contemplation, studying scripture and trying to understand the mysteries of God. However, instead of bringing him peace, this life only deepened his paranoia. He began to believe that God was constantly sending him messages--signs and visions--but that he was failing to interpret them correctly. This fear gnawed at him, driving him to the edge of madness. Yahya became obsessed with deciphering these divine messages, convinced that he was part of some grand, cosmic plan but eternally unsure of his role within it.

To quell his paranoia, Yahya began embracing individuals who claimed to have prophetic abilities--seers, mystics, and visionaries--hoping that through them, he could find the clarity he so desperately sought. These childer were meant to act as conduits between him and God, interpreting the messages that Yahya believed were being sent his way. Yet, every decade or so, the revelations they brought him would fail to satisfy his ever-growing need for understanding. Each failure drove him deeper into madness, as his belief that he was missing some crucial aspect of the divine plan consumed him. He would abandon his childer, moving on to find new prophets to embrace, but none could grant him the answers he sought.

In 1430, feeling once again restless and unfulfilled, Yahya traveled north, leaving behind the monasteries of Byzantium to seek out new forms of wisdom among the witches and seers of the Slavic lands. He believed that the magic and ancient traditions of these lands might offer him a new perspective on God's messages. However, as he wandered through Bohemia, Yahya stumbled upon a **Hussite communion**, a gathering that would change his path once again. In their radical reinterpretation of Christian doctrine and their rebellion against the Catholic Church, Yahya saw echoes of the divine confusion he felt. The Hussites, with their rejection of established authority, resonated with Yahya's belief that God's truth was being misinterpreted and that the established order was blind to the true messages from above.

Convinced that Prague, the heart of the Hussite movement, was where God intended him to be, Yahya settled in the city in 1433. He became fascinated with the Hussites' interpretation of Christian scripture, feeling that perhaps their struggle was the key to unlocking the mystery of God's messages. He interpreted the Hussite rebellion as a sign that Prague was the epicenter of a divine plan he had to uncover. His paranoia deepened once more, as he believed the city itself held the answers he had been searching for across centuries. The religious upheaval and the tensions between Catholicism and the Hussites fed into his belief that this was the battleground where God's true intentions would finally be revealed to him.

To aid in his search, Yahya has begun calling his scattered childer back to Prague. He believes that together, they can piece together the signs and messages that have plagued him for so long. However, many of his childer, having endured decades or even centuries of his madness, are wary of returning. Those who do answer his summons find a Yahya more paranoid and obsessed than ever, convinced that his role in God's plan is on the verge of being revealed. They are drawn into his world of prophetic visions, religious zeal, and divine confusion, as Yahya continues his relentless quest to uncover the truth hidden behind God's cryptic messages.

## Attributes

| Trait | Rating |
|---|---:|
| Strength | 1 |
| Dexterity | 1 |
| Stamina | 3 |
| Charisma | 1 |
| Manipulation | 5 |
| Appearance | 2 |
| Perception | 5 |
| Intelligence | 2 |
| Wits | 3 |

## Abilities

**Talents:** Alertness 3, Athletics 1, Awareness 3, Empathy 4, Expression 1, Intimidation 1, Leadership 2, Subterfuge 2

**Skills:** Etiquette 2, Stealth 2, Survival 2

**Knowledges:** Academics 2, Enigmas 4, Investigation 2, Occult 2

  Sudden Revelations
  
  Characters with even one level of Auspex may occasionally get a sense of an impending threat against them. While they are otherwise using any level of Auspex, the Storyteller may choose to roll for a sudden revelation. In secret, she rolls the character's Auspex score. The difficulty varies by Storyteller discretion, as a robber behind an inn door would probably be difficulty 4, while a plot by the Prince's childe to destroythe character that would take decades to unravel would be difficulty 9. Difficulty can also reflect how important it is to the story that the character has a glimpse of what's coming. Keep in mind though, it is only a brief precognitive glimpse that may or may not make sense before the event unfurls. Divination is not a science; express the revelation in small sensory details or simple gut feelings. "You smell fire with no source coming from the farm house" or "he can't be trusted alone" are excellent examples

Malkavian clan elders claim the conscious mind is a pleasing and soothing mask, and that the Discipline never creates a madness that wasn't already there. Instead, it crumbles the barriers of self-control and consciousness to unleash the deep, dark insanity that dwells beneath. Viewed in this light, Dementation does not corrupt the mind ofthe victim--it unmasks their mind's truth for all to see.
  
  Each level of Dementation may only be used once per night on a victim. While Malkavians are the natural masters of this Discipline, it's usable by any vampire who possesses a Derangement. Vampires learning Dementation will gradually develop a Derangement; if this Derangementis cured by means magical or mundane, their knowledge of Dementation becomes unusable academia. Dwelling on this knowledge presages a relapse into madness.
